note: Canonical policy areas tracked for crosspartisan-convergence analysis. Stance is a -2 to +2 integer scale. Null = no known public position. Definitions are deliberately narrow: a claim must address the specific mechanism described, not just the broad topic.

policy_areas.label: Federal preemption of state AI laws
policy_areas.question: Should Congress preempt state-level AI regulation in favor of a federal framework?
policy_areas.support_means: Favors federal preemption; states should not create their own frontier-AI safety or liability regimes.
policy_areas.oppose_means: States should retain authority to regulate AI, especially when federal law is silent.
policy_areas.expected_convergence: Classic federalism split. Progressive-D state AGs and libertarian-R state officials may both oppose federal preemption; national-security D and big-tech-friendly R may both support it.

policy_areas.label: Restrictions on open-weight frontier models
policy_areas.question: Should the US restrict the release of open-weight frontier model weights?
policy_areas.support_means: Favors restrictions, licensing, or delays on open release of frontier weights above some capability threshold.
policy_areas.oppose_means: Open weights should remain broadly releasable; restrictions would harm research, competition, and democratic accountability.
policy_areas.expected_convergence: Libertarian-R and progressive-D civil-society voices may converge on keeping weights open; national-security D and hawkish R may converge on restriction.

policy_areas.label: Training-compute reporting and thresholds
policy_areas.question: Should training compute (FLOPs) above a threshold trigger mandatory reporting, registration, or licensing?
policy_areas.support_means: Favors a compute-based reporting/licensing regime (e.g., EO 14110-style 10^26 FLOPs threshold or similar).
policy_areas.oppose_means: Opposes compute thresholds as a trigger for regulation; prefers outcome-based or no regulation.
policy_areas.expected_convergence: Accelerationists across the aisle oppose thresholds; safety-focused D and national-security R may converge in favor.

policy_areas.label: AI chip and model export controls to adversaries
policy_areas.question: Should the US tighten export controls on advanced AI chips and/or frontier model weights to adversary states (primarily PRC)?
policy_areas.support_means: Favors tighter controls, closing loopholes, extending to model weights.
policy_areas.oppose_means: Favors loosening or narrowing controls; skeptical they work or worth the economic cost.
policy_areas.expected_convergence: National-security hawks in both parties converge on tightening; industry-friendly voices in both parties may converge on narrower controls.

policy_areas.label: Mandatory pre-deployment evaluations
policy_areas.question: Should frontier models require third-party or government evaluations before public release?
policy_areas.support_means: Favors mandatory pre-deployment testing (red-teaming, capability evals, safety evals) as a condition of release.
policy_areas.oppose_means: Evaluations should remain voluntary; mandatory testing is premature or would entrench incumbents.
policy_areas.expected_convergence: Consumer-protection D and safety-focused R may converge in favor; small-gov R and innovation-focused D may converge against.

policy_areas.label: Developer liability for AI-caused harm
policy_areas.question: Should AI developers bear strict or product liability for downstream harms caused by their models?
policy_areas.support_means: Favors strict or product liability for frontier AI developers; Section 230-style shields should not apply.
policy_areas.oppose_means: Opposes strict liability; harm should be attributed to deployers/users, or existing tort law suffices.
policy_areas.expected_convergence: Trial-lawyer-aligned D and consumer-protection R may converge in favor; tech-friendly D and deregulatory R may converge against.
